notes: BG 2.14 is the titikṣā verse, and the engineering layer preserves the term's technical specificity. Both Shankara (the discipline of not coupling stability to polarity, grounded in Self-realization) and Ramanuja (duty-enabling endurance until duties are discharged) read titikṣā as active discipline, not stoic suppression. The engineering layer's 'refusal to couple stability to polarity' captures the Shankara reading; the implication ('the work — examine, acknowledge, delegate — proceeds through the operator, not over them') captures the Ramanuja reading. The two readings are compatible at the engineering level — duty-enabling endurance and polarity-decoupling are mutually reinforcing — and the engineering layer wisely incorporates both without committing to either ontology of the Self that grounds them. The technical Sanskrit terms āgama/apāya are preserved verbatim with translations in parentheses. The mātrā-sparśāḥ disagreement (Shankara: organs-with-objects; Ramanuja: subtle-elements-via-senses) is preserved at the source-pack level; engineering layer remains at the level both readings accept (sense-contact). PASS.
